sentences.text: In the previous tape, I spoke about my life that took place in a very nice atmosphere.
And I was very pleased, and I took this day to recall with nostalgia those pleasant moments in my life.
Yes.
From that moment on, my mother took upon herself a goal in her life to save her children.
I was a teenager and so was my brother.
My brother just turned 13 years.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the beginning, we lived in our house, but we were cut off from the rest of the world.
We could not go out that much.
And of course we couldn't work, we couldn't earn any money.
So whatever was left from before, we have to make do.
Fortunately, we were in a better position.
Whatever was left, my mother was able to make meet ends from what we had.
She provided for us food, which was the most important thing.
And nothing else we-- we were just surviving from day to day, hoping for a better tomorrow.
After my father left, we did not hear from him.
And somehow we didn't expect to hear from him, hoping that he passed the border and is somewhere in Russia.
But unfortunately it did not happen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a very short while-- I do not recall how long it took-- we were told that my father was killed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I do not recall the details, who told us about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, when he left, he left with horses.
At that time, that was the only mean of transportation.
Those horses were our own in normal times, when my father dealt in the grain business.
And then, when he was in the kolkhoz, he also used the horses.
Two of them.
Big, beautiful horses.
And when he left Warkowicze to go, to flee, he also went with the horses.
Somebody brought one horse back to us, and told us that my father was killed.
It happened that he went as far as the border, was a shtetl by the name of Ostrog.
That was the border between Poland and Russia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The border was guarded, and the guard did not let my father cross.
He remained in Ostrog overnight.
That night the Germans bombed Ostrog and the house where my father was.
My father was killed, and also one horse.
And somehow one horse remained alive.

The person that came to tell us that, also said that where my father was, after the bombs left, and it was quiet, there was a man that lived in the house by the name of Waldman, and he was guarding the body.
According to our religion, a dead body is not supposed to be left alone.
There was-- somebody has to guard it.
And that Mr.
Waldman, a strange man, took it upon himself to stay, even though it was dangerous.
The bombs did not cease to be.
He stayed overnight with my father's body.
And he saw to it that the next day somebody will take the horse and bring it to our town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember that instance, when a strange man came with the horse.
The horse, though an animal, felt the tragedy.
His head was bent, the eyes closed, and when you looked at that horse, you were able to see that he is also crying for the loss.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For us, for my mother, for my brother, and for me, it was like the end of the world for us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was 40 years old, was healthy.
I don't remember a day that my father was sick or complaining about anything.
And then one day, he was gone.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We were in danger, too.
Living under the German occupation, every day brought something else.
Very sad news.
Here, the authorities wanted from the Jews all the gold they possessed.
Then they asked for furs.
Then they asked for silver, for precious metals.
For whatever one had, they asked.
And everybody had to give it.
Otherwise, life was at stake.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a while, a Judenrat was formed in our shtetl.
A Judenrat means that there was like an administration.
There was somebody responsible, that the authorities spoke to them.
They couldn't speak to every Jew separate.
They gave an order to the Judenrat, and the Judenrat told the rest of the people, you must bring your possessions.
Otherwise our life is at stake.
And that's what most people did.
We tried to hide some of the possessions that we had, not to be left without anything.
But basically, it was handed to them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then they formed a closed ghetto.
Half of the city was moved into the other part of the city.
The house that we lived remained in the ghetto.
And another family came to live with us.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We took it in our stride, because we had no alternative.
My mother tried very hard to provide for us the necessities.
And that's how life was from day to day.
I was taken to work every day, near the city of Rivne.
The Germans built an airport, and they took workers there to build it up.
Some people worked on the roads, because our town was on the main road that the German army went from Germany to Russia.
So their heavy equipment broke the roads, and they made sure that the roads are fixed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked on that airport probably for a month.
So every day they picked us up and they brought us there.
And we worked all day long.
And then in the evening, they brought us back home.
After a few weeks of working there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was injured.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my-- all of a sudden I felt a rock hit my head.
And I was bleeding.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took me into an infirmary.
There was a German infirmary there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had very long braids.
So in order for them to see the wound, because I was bleeding profusely, they unbraided the hair.
And the Germans said, [GERMAN], that I  have beautiful hair.
The fact that my life was at stake, he didn't see it.
They couldn't do much because the wound was very deep.
So they sent me to Rivne to the hospital.
And there they stitched it up, and they bandaged the head, and they said that they think-- the doctors there said that they think that I'm out of danger.
And when I came back home, my mother was furious.
What happened to you?
I told her the story, as long as my life was not in danger.
But they did not take me anymore to work.
I stayed home.
Life in ghetto turned out to be harder and harder.
Because how long can people sustain themselves with what they have?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were looking for ways how to survive.
That was the main goal.
I myself was very young, maybe I was too overprotected, to have initiative what to do.
But my mother broke her head, thinking, always thinking, what can she do, how can she protect.
So one day she decided that she will bring to a Gentile place in a little village, and maybe I can be there so that I'll be out of the ghetto.
Now, here I want to explain that we lived on the Ukraine.
Being that our region was so fertile, in the turn of the century, the Russian government-- that was in the late 1800s-- invited people to come and to exploit the fertile Ukrainian soil.
Some Czech settlers came to the Ukraine, and they built a life for themselves.
They prospered.
Their homes were much more nicer than the other Gentiles.
They have more land.
They used more modern equipment, so that they took advantage of that.
They had more grain.
My father dealt with that type of people.
They were very nice people.
It was easy to deal with them.
And my father and my mother had a very good rapport with them.
So they asked one of these Czech families I should go there in a small village and be with them, whatever I can do.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was difficult for me to leave home and be in that environment.
And I was heartbroken on the loss of my father.
The grief did not cease for a minute.
But I realized that I have to go on living, and I did the best I could.
With the Czech family, the chores were I should take the cows out to the pasture.
I was always afraid of a cow, but I had to do it, so I did.
Next door to that Czech family was another Czech family.
And there was a young girl, my age.
I didn't know her.
But she was also on the field with her cow.
So we started to talk.
I asked her about school, about-- I did not go to school then already, but she did.
And we became like friends.
In my mind, I was thinking that if I would have a identification of a Christian girl, if need be, I would have something to show.
I did not look like a typical Jewish girl.
I have a round face, and I had long braids, and I spoke fluently the language.
So the girl told me yes, she has an identification from school.
I said, where do you have it?
She said, in the house.
She took me into the house and showed me in a drawer her identification.
And we kept on talking, and a few days went by, and the girl told me that she is going the next day, her parents are taking her to a big city to buy her a dress.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The houses were not locked.
There was-- nobody was a thief there, not that I know of.
Well, as much as that goes against my grain-- I was brought up with morals, not to steal, not to kill, not to do any harm to anybody, which I never did in my life.
I never even insulted anybody.
But at that moment, I thought to myself, I'm going to take that identification.
Maybe this will help me some time, save my life.
And I sneaked into the house and took that identification, and kept it by me all the time.
And I came home, and I told my mother about it.
My mother cried.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did use that identification at a very crucial time.
It did help.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After awhile, I came home.
I did not stay that long by the Czech woman.
I stayed a few, maybe a few weeks in the summer.
I came back.
I came back.
My mother again was thinking what can she-- how can she protect me?
Somehow I was on my mother's mind even more than my brother.
My brother was very energetic, very vivacious little boy, and I was very quiet and shy.
So probably she thought that somehow he will manage, and I was so helpless.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, the most important thing there, at that time, was whoever stayed out of the ghetto had a better chance to survive.
In our little town was a family by the name of Goldenberg.
There was a young man, a bachelor, Shlomo Goldenberg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was an accountant in a mill outside the ghetto, in another village.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother had a brainstorm.
She didn't even tell me what she was doing, but she did it on her own.
And when she came back she told me.
I was a teenager.
And she thought that that young man, Shlomo Goldenberg, he is alone.
He can make like a fiction, to write in the paper that he has a wife.
Doesn't have to be a wife, but officially I can be a wife and be out of the ghetto.
So she approached Shlomo Goldenberg and asked him to do it.
We knew each other, it's a small town, and everybody knew everybody.
And we were even friends.
But Shlomo told her that his first obligation is to his mother, that he has his mother.
And when he's outside the ghetto, his mother is with him.
So this didn't work.
She came home and she told me this story, and that was the end.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Day-to-day living in the ghetto became harder and harder.
It was scarce food, the people were tense.
They didn't know what one day will bring, and what the next day has in store for us.
We heard what went on.
There were no papers to read, so we really didn't know.
But we heard from mouth to mouth, they were telling us what went on in other cities, how Hitler was killing the Jews.
How and-- where and how, we didn't know.
But we were afraid that maybe that will happen to us too.
So each one was thinking, his aim was, just to get out of the ghetto.
So when that will happen, we will be out of the ghetto so we have a chance to survive.
It was a closed ghetto, yes.
It was a wall around it.
A high gutter was around.
We got out of it because when-- there were two guards on two sides of the ghetto, and this is how we get out.
It was Jewish guards, so they sort of like overlooked whoever went out.
But we were told to wear yellow stars, and each one wore on the front and on the back a yellow star.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents owned the land and a farmer took care of it.
So they told us, that farmer and his wife, that whenever we have a chance to come there, they would give us some food.
We ran out of food.
My mother was going out to get food.
It was very dangerous for her.
So one day I said to her, you know, I don't look Jewish.
I'll take off my yellow stars and I'll go to that farmer.
Maybe I'll bring something, some vegetables.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother was reluctant, but it was a necessity.
So she said, OK.
I went to the farmer, and he gave me quite a bit of vegetables.
And I took it in both my hands and went back home.
I was almost close to the gate, but I heard somebody was screaming in the back.
I made believe that it's not to me.
I didn't turn around, because I saw the gate, and I felt that if I make it close to the gate I'll be safe.
All of a sudden I heard a shooting, and one of the Ukrainian policeman came in front of me and said, I am calling you for a while.
What are you, deaf?
And he slapped me on both sides of my face.
And he said, you are lucky that I don't want to kill you.
Why didn't you stop?
Come with me to the police.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He took me to the police.
He threw away the vegetables there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The police was with-- Ukrainian policeman.
No Germans.
The Germans trusted the Ukrainians.
They were terrible anti-Semites, and they helped.
They even did more than they were told to do.
Came to the police station.
They asked him, what happened?
And he was angry.
He said, you know, I am calling her and she doesn't respond.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were laughing.
They were in a very good mood.
They thought that they have something good now.
All of a sudden, I saw from the Judenrat a man-- that we were related to him, a cousin of my mother's-- came in and spoke to them.
And he said to me, come.
Later I found out that the guards by the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came to my mother and told her what happened.
And my mother ran to the Judenrat, and that cousin of mine went to the station and took me out.
If that would have been further up, if they would have caught me, and the guard wouldn't have seen what happened, they would have probably raped me and killed me, and that would have been the end.
It was a lucky break that I had.
My face was swollen.
It took me a few days til I came back to myself.
But that was some experience for me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since then, of course, I stayed home.
I did not go out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Besides surviving and having the necessities, my mother worried very much that I'm young, and I'm losing time, that I cannot go to school, that I'm not learning anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, a young woman that helped me when I was preparing for the exams to the gymnasium-- she was a university student.
And she was engaged to a young boy from our city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that young boy and she were students in the university in Warsaw.
And for the summer they came to our little town.
They wanted to earn a little money, so I was their student.
They prepared me-- they tutored me to prepare for the exams.
That young woman, was-- her name was Jana, and she was married to Motke Finkiel, that grew up in our shtetl.
And his parents lived there.
He was a law student in Warsaw.
And the day when the war broke out, they were married.
They were planning on getting married September, 1939.
That's when they were married, and the war broke out.
They left Warsaw and came to our shtetl.
And then they realized that the German army are not going to stop in our shtetl.
She helped me-- she and her husband-- at that time he was a fiance-- they were tutoring me to prepare for the exams.
